expanded_description: Mass-to-mass stoichiometric calculations predict the masses of reactants needed or products formed in chemical reactions using molar masses and mole ratios.

core_concept.principle: Mass-to-mass calculations convert between masses of different substances in a reaction using a three-step pathway: mass → moles → moles → mass.
core_concept.explanation: Since chemical equations relate substances in mole ratios (not mass ratios), we must convert given mass to moles, use the mole ratio from the balanced equation, then convert back to mass. This is the most common type of stoichiometry problem in chemistry.

image.alt: Diagram showing the mass-to-mass conversion pathway through moles
image.caption: All mass-to-mass calculations pass through moles using molar mass and mole ratios

sections.introduction: Mass-to-mass calculations are the cornerstone of practical stoichiometry. In the laboratory, we measure substances by mass, but reactions occur in mole ratios. This calculation type bridges that gap.

key_concept.definition: The mass-to-mass pathway: $\text{mass A} \rightarrow \text{mol A} \rightarrow \text{mol B} \rightarrow \text{mass B}$
key_concept.context: This four-step process requires two molar masses (for substances A and B) and one mole ratio (from the balanced equation).

principles.statement: Mass-to-mass stoichiometry requires exactly three conversion steps: (1) mass to moles of given, (2) moles to moles using mole ratio, (3) moles to mass of unknown.
principles.application: Each step uses a different conversion factor: molar mass, mole ratio, then molar mass again.
principles.example: To find mass of $\text{NH}_3$ from mass of $\text{N}_2$: $\text{g } \text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{mol } \text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{mol } \text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{g } \text{NH}_3$
principles.analogy: Like traveling between cities through a hub airport—you can't fly directly, so you go: City A → Hub → City B.

components.definition: Mass-to-mass calculations require three key pieces of information: balanced equation, molar mass of given substance, and molar mass of unknown substance.
components.equation: $\text{mass B} = \text{mass A} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol A}}{\text{molar mass A}} \times \dfrac{\text{mol B}}{\text{mol A}} \times \dfrac{\text{molar mass B}}{1 \text{ mol B}}$
components.interpretation: Each fraction represents one conversion step. Units cancel diagonally, leaving only the desired unit (mass B).

image.alt: Equation diagram showing the mass-to-mass calculation formula
image.caption: The complete mass-to-mass calculation equation with all conversion factors

worked_examples.title: Basic Mass-to-Mass Calculation
worked_examples.problem: How many grams of $\text{NH}_3$ are produced from $10.0$ g of $\text{N}_2$? ($\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NH}_3$)

step_by_step.explanation: Check atom counts: 2 N on both sides, 6 H on both sides ✓
Use molar mass of $\text{N}_2$ ($28.02$ g/mol)

step_by_step.step: Convert moles of $\text{N}_2$ to moles of $\text{NH}_3$
step_by_step.explanation: Use mole ratio from equation: $\dfrac{2 \text{ mol } \text{NH}_3}{1 \text{ mol } \text{N}_2}$
Use molar mass of $\text{NH}_3$ ($17.03$ g/mol)

worked_examples.concept_applied: Mass-to-mass calculations use three conversion steps through moles.

sections.key_concept: Mass-to-mass problems can be solved step-by-step or in one continuous dimensional analysis setup. Both methods give the same answer.

classifications.value: Calculate each conversion separately, writing intermediate values
classifications.behavior: Good for learning and showing work on exams

examples.process: Calculate moles first, then use in next step
examples.explanation: $10.0 \text{ g } \text{N}_2 \rightarrow 0.357 \text{ mol } \text{N}_2 \rightarrow 0.714 \text{ mol } \text{NH}_3 \rightarrow 12.2 \text{ g } \text{NH}_3$

classifications.value: Chain all conversion factors in one calculation

examples.explanation: $10.0 \text{ g } \text{N}_2 \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol } \text{N}_2}{28.02 \text{ g}} \times \dfrac{2 \text{ mol } \text{NH}_3}{1 \text{ mol } \text{N}_2} \times \dfrac{17.03 \text{ g } \text{NH}_3}{1 \text{ mol } \text{NH}_3} = 12.2 \text{ g}$

classifications.value: Calculate mass of one reactant needed to react with given mass of another

examples.explanation: $\text{g } \text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{mol } \text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{mol } \text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{g } \text{H}_2$

classifications.value: Calculate mass of product formed from given mass of reactant

examples.explanation: $\text{g } \text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{mol } \text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{mol } \text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{g } \text{NH}_3$

worked_examples.title: Continuous Dimensional Analysis Setup
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the mass of $\text{O}_2$ needed to react completely with $25.0$ g of $\text{H}_2$ to form water. ($2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$)

step_by_step.explanation: Given: $25.0$ g $\text{H}_2$; Unknown: g $\text{O}_2$
Molar mass $\text{H}_2 = 2.016$ g/mol; Molar mass $\text{O}_2 = 32.00$ g/mol; Mole ratio: $\dfrac{1 \text{ mol } \text{O}_2}{2 \text{ mol } \text{H}_2}$
Chain all three conversions in one calculation
step_by_step.calculation: $? \text{ g } \text{O}_2 = 25.0 \text{ g } \text{H}_2 \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol } \text{H}_2}{2.016 \text{ g } \text{H}_2} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol } \text{O}_2}{2 \text{ mol } \text{H}_2} \times \dfrac{32.00 \text{ g } \text{O}_2}{1 \text{ mol } \text{O}_2}$

step_by_step.explanation: All units cancel except g $\text{O}_2$; multiply across numerator, divide by denominator

worked_examples.concept_applied: Continuous dimensional analysis minimizes rounding errors and shows complete conversion pathway.

worked_examples.title: Mass-to-Mass for Product Formation
worked_examples.problem: What mass of $\text{CO}_2$ is produced when $50.0$ g of propane ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$) burns completely? ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + 5\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 3\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$)

step_by_step.explanation: Chain: g $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$ → mol $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$ → mol $\text{CO}_2$ → g $\text{CO}_2$
step_by_step.calculation: $? \text{ g } \text{CO}_2 = 50.0 \text{ g } \text{C}_3\text{H}_8 \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol } \text{C}_3\text{H}_8}{44.09 \text{ g}} \times \dfrac{3 \text{ mol } \text{CO}_2}{1 \text{ mol } \text{C}_3\text{H}_8} \times \dfrac{44.01 \text{ g } \text{CO}_2}{1 \text{ mol } \text{CO}_2}$

step_by_step.explanation: Multiply and divide, keeping track of significant figures

worked_examples.concept_applied: Combustion reactions use the same mass-to-mass pathway as other reaction types.

sections.introduction: Some mass-to-mass problems involve compounds with complex formulas, hydrates, or polyatomic ions. The calculation pathway remains the same, but molar mass calculations require extra care.

key_concept.definition: Complex formulas require careful molar mass calculation, but the mass-to-mass pathway (mass → mol → mol → mass) remains unchanged.
key_concept.context: Always calculate molar masses accurately before beginning the stoichiometric calculation.

principles.statement: Errors in molar mass calculation propagate through the entire stoichiometric calculation, producing wrong final answers.
principles.application: Double-check molar mass calculations, especially for compounds with parentheses or hydrates.
principles.analogy: Like building a house on a faulty foundation—if the base is wrong, everything above it is wrong too.

categories.description: Hydrated compounds include water molecules in their formula, which must be included in molar mass.
categories.detailed_mechanism: The water molecules contribute to the total molar mass but may or may not participate in the reaction.

examples.explanation: $\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$: molar mass = $249.70$ g/mol (includes 5 water molecules)
$\text{MgSO}_4 \cdot 7\text{H}_2\text{O}$: molar mass = $246.52$ g/mol (includes 7 water molecules)

categories.description: Parentheses indicate multiple polyatomic ions; distribute subscripts correctly when calculating molar mass.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Multiply all atoms inside parentheses by the external subscript before summing.

examples.explanation: Contains 2 Al, 3 S, 12 O; molar mass = $342.17$ g/mol
Contains 3 N, 12 H, 1 P, 4 O; molar mass = $149.10$ g/mol

worked_examples.title: Mass-to-Mass with Hydrated Compound
worked_examples.problem: What mass of anhydrous $\text{CuSO}_4$ is produced from heating $100.0$ g of $\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$? ($\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CuSO}_4 + 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$)

step_by_step.explanation: Mole ratio is 1:1 from balanced equation
step_by_step.calculation: $? \text{ g } \text{CuSO}_4 = 100.0 \text{ g hydrate} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol hydrate}}{249.70 \text{ g}} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol } \text{CuSO}_4}{1 \text{ mol hydrate}} \times \dfrac{159.62 \text{ g } \text{CuSO}_4}{1 \text{ mol } \text{CuSO}_4}$

step_by_step.explanation: Multiply and divide

worked_examples.concept_applied: Hydrate molar mass includes water; anhydrous molar mass does not.

worked_examples.title: Mass-to-Mass with Polyatomic Ions
worked_examples.problem: What mass of $\text{AgCl}$ precipitate forms when $25.0$ g of $\text{CaCl}_2$ reacts with excess $\text{AgNO}_3$? ($\text{CaCl}_2 + 2\text{AgNO}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{AgCl} + \text{Ca}(\text{NO}_3)_2$)

step_by_step.explanation: Mole ratio: $\dfrac{2 \text{ mol } \text{AgCl}}{1 \text{ mol } \text{CaCl}_2}$
step_by_step.calculation: $? \text{ g } \text{AgCl} = 25.0 \text{ g } \text{CaCl}_2 \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol } \text{CaCl}_2}{110.98 \text{ g}} \times \dfrac{2 \text{ mol } \text{AgCl}}{1 \text{ mol } \text{CaCl}_2} \times \dfrac{143.32 \text{ g } \text{AgCl}}{1 \text{ mol } \text{AgCl}}$

step_by_step.explanation: Multiply and divide, round to 3 significant figures

worked_examples.concept_applied: Precipitation reactions use the same mass-to-mass pathway.

sections.key_concept: Multiple verification methods help ensure mass-to-mass calculations are correct before submitting final answers.

classifications.value: Verify all units cancel correctly except the desired final unit
classifications.behavior: If units don't cancel properly, the conversion factors are arranged incorrectly

examples.explanation: $\text{g A} \times \dfrac{\text{mol A}}{\text{g A}} \times \dfrac{\text{mol B}}{\text{mol A}} \times \dfrac{\text{g B}}{\text{mol B}} = \text{g B}$ ✓

classifications.value: Verify the answer magnitude makes chemical sense
classifications.behavior: Compare mole ratios and molar masses to estimate expected answer range

examples.explanation: If mole ratio is 1:2 and molar masses are similar, expect product mass ≈ 2× reactant mass

classifications.value: Verify total mass of reactants equals total mass of products (for complete reactions)
classifications.behavior: Sum of all reactant masses should equal sum of all product masses

examples.explanation: If $10$ g A + $20$ g B react completely, products should total $30$ g

classifications.value: Verify answer has correct number of significant figures
classifications.behavior: Answer should match the least precise given value

examples.explanation: If given $25.0$ g (3 sig figs), answer should have 3 sig figs

worked_examples.title: Complete Verification of Mass-to-Mass Calculation
worked_examples.problem: Verify this calculation: $15.0$ g $\text{H}_2$ produces $134$ g $\text{NH}_3$. ($\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NH}_3$)

step_by_step.explanation: Verify setup: $\text{g } \text{H}_2 \times \dfrac{\text{mol } \text{H}_2}{\text{g } \text{H}_2} \times \dfrac{\text{mol } \text{NH}_3}{\text{mol } \text{H}_2} \times \dfrac{\text{g } \text{NH}_3}{\text{mol } \text{NH}_3} = \text{g } \text{NH}_3$ ✓
Use molar masses: $\text{H}_2 = 2.016$ g/mol, $\text{NH}_3 = 17.03$ g/mol; mole ratio: $\dfrac{2 \text{ mol } \text{NH}_3}{3 \text{ mol } \text{H}_2}$
Calculated $84.5$ g vs. given $134$ g—discrepancy found!
Given answer likely used wrong mole ratio (possibly $\dfrac{3}{2}$ instead of $\dfrac{2}{3}$)

worked_examples.final_answer: Given answer ($134$ g) is incorrect; correct answer is $84.5$ g $\text{NH}_3$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Verification catches errors before they become final answers.

worked_examples.title: Reasonableness Check Example
worked_examples.problem: A student calculated that $10.0$ g of $\text{Mg}$ produces $500$ g of $\text{MgO}$. Is this reasonable? ($2\text{Mg} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{MgO}$)

step_by_step.explanation: Mole ratio is 1:1 (2:2 simplifies); molar mass $\text{Mg} ≈ 24$ g/mol, $\text{MgO} ≈ 40$ g/mol
Student got $500$ g; estimate is $17$ g—huge discrepancy!
Student may have multiplied instead of divided, or used wrong molar mass
Use proper dimensional analysis
step_by_step.calculation: $? = 10.0 \text{ g } \text{Mg} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol } \text{Mg}}{24.31 \text{ g}} \times \dfrac{2 \text{ mol } \text{MgO}}{2 \text{ mol } \text{Mg}} \times \dfrac{40.30 \text{ g } \text{MgO}}{1 \text{ mol } \text{MgO}} = 16.6 \text{ g}$

worked_examples.final_answer: Not reasonable; correct answer is $16.6$ g $\text{MgO}$, not $500$ g
worked_examples.concept_applied: Reasonableness checks catch major errors quickly before detailed verification.

sections.key_concept: Understanding common mistakes in mass-to-mass calculations helps ensure accurate results.

classifications.value: Attempting to convert directly from mass of A to mass of B
classifications.behavior: Produces answers off by factors of molar mass; must go through moles

examples.process: Wrong: g A → g B directly
examples.explanation: Cannot skip moles; must use: g A → mol A → mol B → g B
Three conversion steps required for mass-to-mass stoichiometry

classifications.value: Using mole ratios from unbalanced chemical equations
classifications.behavior: Always balance the equation before starting any stoichiometric calculation

examples.explanation: Gives 1:1 $\text{H}_2$:$\text{H}_2\text{O}$ ratio instead of correct 2:2 (or 1:1) ratio
Gives correct 2:2 $\text{H}_2$:$\text{H}_2\text{O}$ ratio

classifications.value: Using atomic mass instead of molecular/compound molar mass
classifications.behavior: Results in systematically wrong answers, often off by factors of 2 or more

examples.explanation: Oxygen gas is $\text{O}_2$ ($32.00$ g/mol), not O ($16.00$ g/mol)
Hydrogen gas is $\text{H}_2$ ($2.016$ g/mol), not H ($1.008$ g/mol)

classifications.value: Using mole ratio in wrong direction
characteristics: Units may cancel but answer is reciprocal
classifications.behavior: Produces answers that are too large or too small by the ratio factor squared

examples.process: Wrong: $\dfrac{1 \text{ mol } \text{N}_2}{2 \text{ mol } \text{NH}_3}$ when converting $\text{N}_2$ to $\text{NH}_3$
examples.explanation: Should use $\dfrac{2 \text{ mol } \text{NH}_3}{1 \text{ mol } \text{N}_2}$ to cancel mol $\text{N}_2$

classifications.value: Rounding numbers at each step instead of at the end
classifications.behavior: Keep extra digits during calculation; round only final answer

examples.process: Wrong: round moles to 2 sig figs, then use in next step
examples.explanation: Should keep 4+ digits during calculation, round final answer only

worked_examples.title: Identifying and Correcting Mass-to-Mass Errors
worked_examples.problem: A student calculated that $20.0$ g of $\text{CH}_4$ produces $20.0$ g of $\text{CO}_2$ in combustion. Identify and correct the errors. ($\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$)

step_by_step.explanation: Student assumed mass is conserved for individual substances (it's not—total mass is conserved, not individual substance mass)
Mole ratio is 1:1 from balanced equation
step_by_step.calculation: $? \text{ g } \text{CO}_2 = 20.0 \text{ g } \text{CH}_4 \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol } \text{CH}_4}{16.04 \text{ g}} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol } \text{CO}_2}{1 \text{ mol } \text{CH}_4} \times \dfrac{44.01 \text{ g } \text{CO}_2}{1 \text{ mol } \text{CO}_2}$

step_by_step.explanation: Multiply and divide
(1) Assumed individual mass conservation; (2) Didn't use mole ratios; (3) Didn't use molar masses

worked_examples.final_answer: Correct: $54.9$ g $\text{CO}_2$. Errors: assumed individual mass conservation, skipped stoichiometry steps.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Individual substance masses change in reactions; only total mass is conserved.

worked_examples.title: Problem-Solving Strategy: Mass-to-Mass Checklist
worked_examples.problem: Outline a systematic approach for all mass-to-mass stoichiometry problems.

step_by_step.step: Step 1: Write and balance the chemical equation
step_by_step.explanation: Never skip—unbalanced equations give wrong mole ratios

step_by_step.step: Step 2: Identify given mass and unknown mass
step_by_step.explanation: Clearly label what you have and what you need to find

step_by_step.step: Step 3: Calculate molar masses for both substances
step_by_step.explanation: Use periodic table; include all atoms (watch for subscripts and parentheses)
Plan: g given → mol given → mol unknown → g unknown
Chain all three conversion factors with proper unit cancellation
Compute answer, check significant figures, verify reasonableness
Verify only desired unit remains; all others cancel

worked_examples.final_answer: Follow the 7-step checklist for all mass-to-mass problems
worked_examples.concept_applied: Systematic approach prevents common mass-to-mass calculation errors.

summary: Mass-to-mass stoichiometric calculations convert between masses of different substances in a chemical reaction using a three-step pathway: (1) convert given mass to moles using molar mass, (2) convert moles of given to moles of unknown using mole ratio from balanced equation, (3) convert moles of unknown to mass using molar mass. The complete equation is: $\text{mass B} = \text{mass A} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol A}}{\text{molar mass A}} \times \dfrac{\text{mol B}}{\text{mol A}} \times \dfrac{\text{molar mass B}}{1 \text{ mol B}}$. Calculations can be done step-by-step or in one continuous dimensional analysis setup (preferred to minimize rounding errors). Complex formulas (hydrates, polyatomic ions) require careful molar mass calculation but use the same pathway. Always verify calculations by checking unit cancellation, reasonableness of answer magnitude, and significant figures. Common errors include skipping the mole step, using unbalanced equations, wrong molar masses, inverted mole ratios, and rounding intermediate values. A systematic 7-step checklist ensures accurate mass-to-mass calculations. Mastery of this calculation type is essential for predicting reactant requirements, product yields, and all practical stoichiometric applications in laboratory and industrial settings.
